explanation: Solutions having same osmotic pressure are known as isotonic solutions. They have the same concentration of the solute.

content: Van't Hoff factor for $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \cdot 10 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ solution, assuming complete ionization is
explanation:
The molecule $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \cdot 10 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ dissociates as follows:

$\bullet$ $2 \mathrm{Na}^{+}$

$\bullet$ $1 \mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$

$\bullet$ $10 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$

Since water is a non-electrolyte, it does not ionize and does not contribute to the van't Hoff factor value.

content: 1 mole of liquid A and 2 moles of liquid $$B$$ make a solution having a total vapour pressure 40 torr. The vapour pressure of pure $$A$$ and pure $$B$$ are 45 torr and 30 torr respectively. The above solution
explanation: Shows negative de viation. because A $$-$$ B forces of attraction are higher than $$\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{A}$$ and $$\mathrm{B}-\mathrm{B}$$ components.

content: If molality of a dilute solution is doubled, the value of the molal elevation constant $$\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}\right)$$ will be
explanation: $$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}$$ is molal elevation constant, when molality is double, it remains unchanged.

content:
(a) (i) Ishan's automobile radiator is filled with 1.0 kg of water. How many grams of ethylene glycol (Molar mass $=62 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ) must Ishan add to get the freezing point of the solution lowered to $-2.8^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}$ for water is $1.86{\mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} . \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \text {. }}$

(ii) What type of deviation from Raoult's law is shown by ethanol and acetone mixture? Give reason.

OR

(b) (i) Boiling point of water at 750 mm Hg pressure is $99.68{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. How much sucrose (Molar mass $=342 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ) is to be added to 500 g of water such that it boils at $100{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ? $\left(\mathrm{K}_b\right.$ for water $=$ $0.52 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ).

(ii) State Henry's law and write its any one application.
explanation:
(a) (i) Lowering the Freezing Point with Ethylene Glycol

To calculate the amount of ethylene glycol needed to lower the freezing point to $-2.8^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, we use the formula for freezing point depression:

$$ \Delta T_f = i \cdot K_f \cdot m $$

where:

$\Delta T_f$ is the change in freezing point.

$i$ is the van 't Hoff factor, which is 1 for ethylene glycol (a non-electrolyte).

$K_f$ is the freezing point depression constant, given as $1.86 \mathrm{~K~kg~mol}^{-1}$.

$m$ is the molality of the solution.

Given that $\Delta T_f = 0^{\circ}\mathrm{C} - (-2.8^{\circ}\mathrm{C}) = 2.8^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$.

Rearranging the formula to find $m$:

$$ m = \frac{\Delta T_f}{i \cdot K_f} = \frac{2.8}{1 \cdot 1.86} = 1.505 \mathrm{~mol~kg}^{-1} $$

Now, calculate the grams of ethylene glycol needed:

Molality ($m$) is defined as moles of solute per kilogram of solvent. Given the solution consists of 1 kg of water, the moles of ethylene glycol required is 1.505 mol.

Mass of ethylene glycol:

$$ \text{mass} = \text{moles} \times \text{molar mass} = 1.505 \, \text{mol} \times 62 \, \text{g/mol} = 93.31 \, \text{g} $$

Therefore, Ishan needs to add 93.31 grams of ethylene glycol.

(a) (ii) Deviation in Ethanol and Acetone Mixture

A mixture of ethanol and acetone shows positive deviation from Raoult's law. This is because the interaction between ethanol and acetone molecules is weaker than the interactions in the pure components. Ethanol forms hydrogen bonds, and when mixed with acetone, these hydrogen bonds are disrupted and replaced with weaker van der Waals interactions.

(b) (i) Raising the Boiling Point with Sucrose

To determine the amount of sucrose required to raise the boiling point to $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, use the equation for boiling point elevation:

$$ \Delta T_b = i \cdot K_b \cdot m $$

where:

$\Delta T_b = 100^{\circ} \mathrm{C} - 99.68^{\circ} \mathrm{C} = 0.32^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

$i = 1$ for sucrose, a non-electrolyte.

$K_b = 0.52 \mathrm{~K~kg~mol}^{-1}$.

Solving for molality $m$:

$$ m = \frac{\Delta T_b}{i \cdot K_b} = \frac{0.32}{1 \cdot 0.52} = 0.615 \mathrm{~mol~kg}^{-1} $$

Given 500 g (0.5 kg) of water:

Moles of sucrose required:

$$ \text{moles} = m \cdot \text{kg of solvent} = 0.615 \, \text{mol/kg} \times 0.5 \, \text{kg} = 0.3075 \, \text{mol} $$

Mass of sucrose:

$$ \text{mass} = \text{moles} \times \text{molar mass} = 0.3075 \, \text{mol} \times 342 \, \text{g/mol} = 105.97 \, \text{g} $$

105.97 grams of sucrose should be added.

(b) (ii) Henry's Law and Application

Henry's Law states that the solubility of a gas in a liquid at a fixed temperature is directly proportional to the partial pressure of the gas above the liquid:

$$ p = k_{\text{H}} \cdot c $$

where $p$ is the partial pressure of the gas, $k_{\text{H}}$ is the Henry's law constant, and $c$ is the concentration of the gas.

Application: Henry's law is used in carbonated beverages to dissolve carbon dioxide under pressure, ensuring that beverages remain fizzy until opened, when the pressure is released.

content:
(a) (i) Why is boiling point of $$1 \mathrm{~M} \mathrm{~NaCl}$$ solution more than that of $$1 \mathrm{M}$$ glucose solution?

(ii) A nonvolatile solute '$$\mathrm{X}$$' (molar mass $$=\mathbf{5 0} \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$$) when dissolved in $$78 \mathrm{~g}$$ of benzene reduced its vapour pressure to $$90 \%$$.

Calculate the mass of $$\mathrm{X}$$ dissolved in the solution.

(iii) Calculate the boiling point elevation for a solution prepared by adding $$10 \mathrm{~g}$$ of $$\mathrm{MgCl}_2$$ to $$200 \mathrm{~g}$$ of water assuming $$\mathrm{MgCl}_2$$ is completely dissociated.

($$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}$$ for Water $$=0.512 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$$, Molar mass $$\mathrm{MgCl}_2=95 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$$)

OR

(b) (i) Why is the value of Van't Hoff factor for ethanoic acid in benzene close to 0.5 ?

(ii) Determine the osmotic pressure of a solution prepared by dissolving $$2.32 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~g}$$ of $$\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$$ in $$2 \mathrm{~L}$$ of solution at $$25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$, assuming that $$\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$$ is completely dissociated.

($$\mathrm{R}=0.082 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~atm} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$$, Molar mass $$\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 =174 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$$)

(iii) When $$25.6 \mathrm{~g}$$ of sulphur was dissolved in $$1000 \mathrm{~g}$$ of benzene, the freezing point lowered by $$0.512 \mathrm{~K}$$. Calculate the formula of sulphur ($$\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{x}}$$). ($$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}$$ for benzene $$=5.12 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$$, Atomic mass of Sulphur $$=32 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$$ )
explanation:
(a) (i) $$\mathrm{NaCl}$$ is having ionic bonding between sodium and chloride atoms which is strong bonding while glucose having covalent bonding which is weak in nature. $$\mathrm{NaCl}$$ will required high temperature to boil while glucose need low temperature to dissociate its bonding.

(ii)

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{P}^{\circ}-\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{s}}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{s}}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}}{\mathrm{N}}=\frac{\mathrm{w} \times \mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{m} \times \mathrm{w}} \\ & \frac{100-90}{90}=\frac{\mathrm{w} \times 78}{50 \times 78} \\ & \frac{10}{90}=\frac{\mathrm{w}}{50} \\ & 90 \times \mathrm{w}=10 \times 50 \\ & \mathrm{w}=\frac{10 \times 50}{90}=5.55 \mathrm{~grams} \end{aligned}$$

(ii)

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{i} \times \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{b}} \times \mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{MgCl}_2=3 \text { ions }\right) \mathrm{i}=3 \\ & \mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{3 \times 0.512 \times 10}{95 \times 0.2}(200 \mathrm{gm}=0.2 \mathrm{Kg}) \\ & =\frac{5.36}{19}=0.80 \end{aligned}$$

So elevation is boiling point $$=273+0.80=273.80$$ K

OR

(b) (i) Van't Hoff factor is used in determination of colligative property. It is also called Abnormal colligative property.

$$i=\frac{\text { actual no. of particle or cocentration }}{\text { Theoretical number of particles }}$$

$$\because$$ It is considered that degree of dissociation of ethanoic acid in benzene is 1

$$2 \mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{COOH} \longrightarrow\left(\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{COOH}\right)_2$$

So, $$i=\frac{1}{2}=0.5$$

(ii) $$\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{~K}^{+}+\mathrm{SO}_4{ }^{2-}$$

ions produced are $$=3$$

$$\begin{aligned} & i=3 \\ & x=i \text { CRT } \\ & =3 \times \frac{0.0232}{174} \times 0.5 \times 0.0821 \times 298 \\ & =\frac{0.851}{174}=0.00489 \\ & =4.89 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~atm} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { (iii) } \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \times \mathrm{m} \times 1000}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}} \times \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{A}}}\left[\because \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}=\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{x}}\right] . \\ & 0.512=\frac{5.12 \times 25.6 \times 1000}{\mathrm{~S}_{\mathrm{x}} \times 1000} \\ & \mathrm{~S}_{\mathrm{x}}=\frac{5.12 \times 25.6}{0.512} \\ & \mathrm{~S}_{\mathrm{x}}=256 \\ & \mathrm{x} \times 32=256 \\ & \mathrm{x}=\frac{256}{32}=8 \end{aligned}$$

So, the required formula (S$$_x$$)

x = 8

or S$$_8$$.
